questions.question_text: In Florida, what is the default brokerage relationship if no written agreement is established?
questions.explanation: Florida is one of the few states where the default brokerage relationship is transaction broker — not single agent. Unless the broker and customer agree otherwise in writing, the broker operates as a transaction broker.

questions.question_text: A Florida transaction broker owes how many duties to the customer?
questions.explanation: A transaction broker owes 7 duties: dealing honestly and fairly, accounting for funds, skill/care/diligence, presenting all offers, limited confidentiality, disclosure of material facts, and limited representation. A single agent owes 9 duties.

questions.question_text: Which two fiduciary duties does a transaction broker NOT owe to the customer?
questions.explanation: A transaction broker does NOT owe loyalty or obedience — these are single agent duties only. This is the key difference: a single agent has 9 duties (including loyalty and obedience), while a transaction broker has 7.

questions.question_text: When must the Transaction Broker Notice be provided to the customer?
questions.correct_answer: Before or at the time of entering the transaction
distractors: Within 3 business days of showing property
At the closing table before signing the deed
Only if the customer specifically requests it
questions.explanation: The Transaction Broker Notice must be given before or at the time of entering into a transaction. All brokerage relationship notices must be in writing and acknowledged by the customer.

questions.question_text: A transaction broker provides what level of representation to the parties?
questions.explanation: A transaction broker provides limited representation — not full fiduciary duties. The broker facilitates the transaction but does not advocate for either party with the loyalty a single agent would provide.

questions.question_text: How many duties does a Florida single agent owe to the principal?
questions.explanation: A single agent owes 9 duties: loyalty, confidentiality, obedience, full disclosure, accounting, skill/care/diligence, presenting all offers, dealing honestly and fairly. Two more than a transaction broker's 7 duties.

questions.question_text: A broker enters a single agent relationship with a seller. Which duty requires the broker to follow the seller's lawful instructions?
questions.explanation: Obedience requires the single agent to follow the principal's lawful instructions. This duty, along with loyalty, distinguishes single agent from transaction broker relationships.

questions.question_text: A listing broker acting as single agent for the seller learns the buyer's maximum budget. What must the broker do?
distractors: Keep it confidential from both parties
questions.explanation: A single agent owes full disclosure and loyalty to the principal (seller). Information about the buyer's maximum budget is material and must be shared with the seller as part of the broker's fiduciary duty.

questions.question_text: The Single Agent Notice must be provided to the principal at what point?
questions.correct_answer: Before or at the time the relationship begins
distractors: At the time of contract signing
Only when requested by the client
questions.explanation: The Single Agent Notice must be given before or at the time the brokerage relationship begins. It must be in writing, and the principal must sign or initial to acknowledge receipt.

questions.question_text: A broker acting as a single agent for the seller wants to also represent the buyer. What must happen first?
questions.correct_answer: Transition to transaction broker with written consent
distractors: Obtain FREC approval for dual agency
Notify DBPR of the dual representation
questions.explanation: Since Florida prohibits dual agency, the broker must transition from single agent to transaction broker. This requires written consent from the party giving up single agent status (the seller in this case).

questions.question_text: Under a no brokerage relationship in Florida, the broker owes which duties to the customer?
questions.correct_answer: Honest dealing, material facts, and accounting
questions.explanation: In a no brokerage relationship, the broker provides factual information only. The three duties owed are: dealing honestly and fairly, disclosure of material facts, and accounting for funds.

questions.question_text: A single agent wants to transition to a transaction broker. What document is required?
questions.explanation: The Consent to Transition form must be signed by the party giving up single agent status BEFORE the change takes effect. This ensures informed consent to the reduced duties.

questions.question_text: Florida handles situations where both buyer and seller work with the same brokerage by using which model?
questions.explanation: Florida does NOT permit dual agency. Instead, when both parties work with the same brokerage, the broker acts as a transaction broker providing limited representation to both without full fiduciary duties to either.

questions.question_text: When must the No Brokerage Relationship notice be given to the customer?
distractors: At the time of the offer
questions.explanation: The No Brokerage Relationship notice must be given before showing property. Like all brokerage notices in Florida, it must be in writing and acknowledged by the customer.

questions.question_text: Which brokerage arrangement is explicitly prohibited in Florida?
questions.explanation: Florida explicitly prohibits dual agency, where one broker represents both buyer and seller with full fiduciary duties. Instead, Florida uses the transaction broker model to handle situations involving both parties.
